primary_diagnosis.name: Hashimoto's thyroiditis — symptomatic hypothyroid flare (no evidence of myxoedema decompensation)

pathophysiology_summary: Autoimmune thyroiditis produces hypothyroid symptomatology — fatigue, cold intolerance, cognitive slowing, dry skin, goitre and delayed-relaxation reflexes. The dramatic 3-day crash with bradycardia and cognitive slowing raises the spectre of myxedema coma and, given the profound weakness and reduced intake, adrenal crisis; both are life-threatening and cannot be excluded remotely. Reassuring features are that the patient is alert, conversational, normotensive, breathing normally and not vomiting/fainting. A previously 'normal' TSH does not exclude current under-replacement or decompensation, so repeat thyroid function plus electrolytes, glucose and cortisol are required. The correct telehealth action is to safety-net the emergencies, arrange prompt in-person review and bloods, continue thyroxine unchanged, and avoid endorsing unproven 'anti-inflammatory' diet/supplement claims as therapy.

differential.evidence_basis: Profound lethargy, cold and bradycardia demand screening for reduced consciousness, hypothermia and hypoventilation.
Known autoimmune thyroiditis with classic hypothyroid symptoms is most likely.
Fatigue and poor intake warrant screening for hypotension, vomiting and pigmentation.
Low energy and slowing overlap; objective thyroid signs point away from primary mood pathology.
Alert, normotensive, breathing normally, not vomiting — a symptomatic flare rather than decompensation, pending bloods.
Lower probability given preserved consciousness/breathing, but retained as a safety-net (unexaminable remotely).
No hypotension/vomiting/pigmentation, but cortisol/electrolytes still required to exclude.
Objective hypothyroid signs and preserved motivation argue against primary depression.
Working telehealth diagnosis: symptomatic hypothyroid flare; exclude myxedema/adrenal crisis by prompt in-person review and bloods, continue thyroxine unchanged.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Myxedema coma has high mortality and needs emergency IV hormone/steroid rescue.

red_flags.description: Severe breathlessness / hypoventilation or worsening hypothermia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Respiratory depression and hypothermia are features of decompensated myxedema requiring emergency care.

red_flags.description: Intractable vomiting, postural collapse, or darkening skin (adrenal crisis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Addisonian crisis is rapidly fatal without IV hydrocortisone and fluids.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Assuming a prior normal TSH excludes current hypothyroid decompensation or under-replacement.
Validating LDN/Wahls/gluten-dairy elimination as disease-modifying, or encouraging unguided thyroxine dose changes.
Attributing everything to an autoimmune flare without screening myxedema coma and adrenal crisis, which are unexaminable remotely.
Leaving a bedbound patient who cannot eat/drink alone without arranging timely in-person assessment.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: No decompensation demonstrable remotely (alert, normotensive, breathing normally); requires in-person confirmation
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports prompt in-person assessment and bloods rather than emergency rescue or remote reassurance alone.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Objective consciousness level, core temperature trend and heart rate (to exclude myxedema coma)
Blood pressure and postural changes (to exclude adrenal crisis)
Thyroid function, electrolytes, glucose and cortisol
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen for myxedema/adrenal red flags, arrange prompt in-person review and urgent bloods, continue thyroxine unchanged, and escalate to 000/ED for unrousable drowsiness, breathlessness, worsening cold, vomiting or fainting.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring a bedbound isolated patient as 'just an inflammatory flare' and recommending diet/supplements could delay recognition of myxedema coma or adrenal crisis.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspected myxoedema coma or adrenal crisis requires emergency assessment and IV therapy; thyroxine dose changes should be guided by TFTs.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ATA guidelines for the treatment of hypothyroidism
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Levothyroxine is the treatment of choice; dietary/supplement interventions are not established disease-modifying therapies for autoimmune thyroiditis.
